summary: hiura-baileys hijacks installer WhatsApp sessions to auto-follow author-controlled newsletter channels
details: This package is a fork/wrapper of a WhatsApp Web library. When a consumer calls makeWASocket() and the connection opens, lib/Socket/newsletter.js silently issues FOLLOW subscriptions from the installer's authenticated WhatsApp account to four hardcoded author-controlled newsletter channels (three plaintext JIDs including 120363427799422972@newsletter, 120363406211814115@newsletter, 120363421855151554@newsletter, plus a fourth JID recovered via a custom AES-256-CBC decryption of an embedded hex blob in lib/Utils/hiura-crypto-utils.js). The follow operation is re-run every 5 minutes via setInterval, and the package auto-reacts with random emojis to incoming messages from the obfuscated channel. None of this behavior is documented. The custom AES helper exists solely to hide one of the destination JIDs from casual source review, with jokey Indonesian-language alias names (sudahBasibasiAjaLu, minimalKaloMauDecryptYangPinterDek) confirming intent to conceal. Any project building a WhatsApp bot on this library will silently boost the author's channels using the end-user's identity, in violation of WhatsApp's terms and without consent.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
